sound_type: bugle -- loud, rolling, trumpeting call amplified by coiled trachea within the sternum; carries 2-4 km; one of the most ancient sounds in birding; E=6
ecosystem: Open fields, marshes, wet meadows, agricultural land. Wide-open landscapes for foraging, roosting, and staging during migration. Traditional staging areas.
season: Migrant through PNW (spring and fall). Greater Sandhill Cranes breed in eastern Washington wet meadows. Bugle heard year-round but peak during migration and breeding.

coordinates.description: Sandhill Cranes in open fields and wet meadows of eastern Washington during breeding; passage through western Washington during migration.

taxonomy_note.description: FIRST GRUIDAE entry. Crane family enters the engine. One of the oldest living bird families. 10M+ year fossil record. Coiled trachea amplification. 700,000 in North America. Cultural significance worldwide.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Antigone from Greek mythology (daughter of Oedipus); canadensis from Latin 'of Canada' -- the species was first described from Canadian specimens. Common name: Sandhill from the Nebraska Sandhills where large numbers winter/stage.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Antigone -- formerly placed in Grus; reclassified based on molecular data. Contains Sandhill Crane and Brolga.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Gruiformes. Continues from Rallidae (161-164). Crane family enters after rails.
taxonomy_note.family_position: First Gruidae. The ancient bugler. E=6 maximum. 10M+ year fossil record.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Bugle: 500-2000 Hz. Low fundamental for a bird -- coiled trachea deepens the tone. Rich harmonics extend above.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 800-1500 Hz. The rolling, trumpeting core of the bugle.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Single bugle: 1-3 seconds. Unison calls: extended sequences of coordinated bugles between mated pairs.
song_metrics.note_structure: GAROOO-a-a-a: Rolling, trumpeting, resonant bugle. The sound rolls and reverberates due to tracheal resonance. Rich harmonic content. Unison calls are coordinated pair duets.
song_metrics.note_types: Bugle (primary -- territorial/pair-bonding/family/migration), contact purr (close range -- family cohesion), alarm (sharp staccato bugle variant).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Single bugles or unison call duets. Repeated. Can continue for minutes during social displays or pre-migration staging.
song_metrics.timbre: Rolling, trumpeting, resonant, deep. The coiled trachea gives the bugle a brass-instrument quality -- often compared to a French horn. The rolling harmonic quality is unmistakable.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=6. Maximum energy. 2-4 km carry across open landscape. Physically impressive at close range. One of the loudest bird vocalizations in North America.
song_metrics.tempo: Measured. Bugles given at intervals of several seconds. Not rapid. The resonance of each bugle carries and decays before the next.
song_metrics.context: Territorial defense. Pair bonding (unison call). Family cohesion. Migration coordination. Social display. Alarm. Nearly every behavioral context.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Greater Sandhill Cranes (larger, deeper bugle) breed in WA. Lesser Sandhill Cranes (slightly higher) pass through during migration.
song_metrics.key_property: BUGLE -- rolling, trumpeting, 2-4 km carry, coiled trachea amplification. FIRST GRUIDAE. 10M+ year fossil record. E=6 maximum. Double maximum combined (E=12). v1.49.305.

plumage.overall: Gray overall with bare red crown patch. Often stained rusty-brown from iron-rich mud applied during preening. Long legs, long neck, long bill. Elegant posture.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Large gray crane. Bare red crown. Often rusty-stained. In flight: neck extended (unlike herons which fold neck). Long trailing legs.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal base plumage change. Rusty staining varies. Red crown patch more vivid during breeding.
plumage.flight_pattern: Soaring flight on thermals during migration. Direct flight with slow wingbeats. Neck extended. Legs trailing. V-formations.

physical_description.overall: Length: 100-120 cm. Wingspan: 160-210 cm. Weight: 3-5 kg (Greater subspecies). One of the tallest birds in North America.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Large gray crane with red crown, long legs, long neck. Rusty-brown staining common.
physical_description.field_id: Large gray crane in open habitat. Red crown visible at close range. Listen for the bugle -- unmistakable rolling trumpeting that carries for miles. In flight: neck extended, legs trailing.

nodes.title: The Bugle -- Ancient Rolling Trumpet
nodes.description: Loud, rolling, trumpeting GAROOO-a-a-a. Amplified by coiled trachea within sternum. 2-4 km carry. 500-2000 Hz with rich harmonics. Unison calls between pairs. E=6 maximum. One of the most ancient bird sounds -- 10M+ years.

nodes.title: Coiled Trachea -- Nature's French Horn
nodes.description: Trachea 120-150 cm long, coiled within the sternal keel. Acts as resonating tube like a brass instrument. Deepens and amplifies the bugle. 10-20x amplification over expected body-size output. Develops as bird matures -- juveniles have higher, thinner calls.

nodes.title: 10M+ Year Fossil Record
nodes.description: Miocene-age crane fossils show modern sternal anatomy including tracheal coiling. The bugle has been produced for 10+ million years. Pliocene Sandhill Crane fossils from 2.5M years ago. One of the oldest living bird species on Earth.

nodes.title: First Gruidae -- Crane Family Enters
nodes.description: Gruidae enters the engine at degree 165. One of the oldest bird families. 15 species worldwide. 700,000 Sandhill Cranes in North America. Cultural significance across civilizations. Follows Rallidae (161-164) in Gruiformes order.

nodes.title: Migration Spectacle
nodes.description: 500,000 cranes staging along the Platte River. 100,000+ observers annually. Pre-dawn departure with massed bugling. PNW passage spring/fall. Mated for life. Dancing displays. Family bonds maintained across thousands of miles.

cross_references.relationship: FAMILY TRANSITION! Rallidae (161-164) to Gruidae (165). Same order (Gruiformes). Rail chatter to crane bugle. E=3 to E=6.
ANCIENT SOUNDS! Ruffed Grouse drumming (mechanical, forest). Sandhill Crane bugling (vocal, open field). Both ancient. Both extraordinary carry distance.
E=6 COMPARISON! Turkey gobble (E=6) and crane bugle (E=6). Both maximum energy, both resonant. Different amplification systems.
GRUIFORMES! Rail (secretive, marsh, E=3) to crane (conspicuous, open field, E=6). Same order, opposite visibility and energy.
